subject: EU Law
module_title: History and Development of the EU
lesson_title: From Rome to Lisbon: The Constitutional Evolution of the Treaties

questions.question: The Single European Act 1986 is significant because it:

options.A: Established the European Union and introduced EU citizenship
options.B: Completed the internal market programme and extended qualified majority voting in the Council
options.C: Created the euro and the European Central Bank
options.D: Incorporated the European Convention on Human Rights into EU law

questions.explanation: The Single European Act (in force 1987) set the target of completing the internal market by 31 December 1992, extended qualified majority voting (QMV) to internal market legislation, enhanced the role of the European Parliament, and formally recognised the European Council.

questions.question: The Maastricht Treaty (Treaty on European Union), which entered into force in 1993, is most significant for:

options.A: Abolishing the three-pillar structure and giving the EU legal personality
options.B: Establishing the European Union, introducing EU citizenship, and creating the three-pillar structure
options.C: Extending the ordinary legislative procedure to all areas of EU law
options.D: Incorporating the Charter of Fundamental Rights as legally binding

questions.explanation: The Maastricht Treaty (TEU, in force November 1993) created the European Union, introduced EU citizenship, established Economic and Monetary Union (leading to the euro), and created the three-pillar structure: Pillar 1 (Communities), Pillar 2 (CFSP), Pillar 3 (Justice and Home Affairs).

questions.question: Under the three-pillar structure established by the Maastricht Treaty, which pillar governed Common Foreign and Security Policy?
questions.explanation: The three pillars were: Pillar 1 — the three Communities (EEC/EC, ECSC, Euratom), governed by supranational law; Pillar 2 — Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP), intergovernmental; Pillar 3 — Justice and Home Affairs, intergovernmental. The Court of Justice had jurisdiction primarily over Pillar 1.

questions.question: The Treaty of Amsterdam (1997, in force 1999) transferred which area from the third pillar to the first pillar?

options.B: Asylum, immigration, and judicial cooperation in civil matters
options.C: Police cooperation and judicial cooperation in criminal matters

questions.explanation: Amsterdam 'communitarised' part of the third pillar: asylum, immigration, and judicial cooperation in civil matters moved to the EC Treaty (first pillar), becoming subject to supranational law and Court of Justice jurisdiction. Police and judicial cooperation in criminal matters remained in the third pillar (renamed Provisions on Police and Judicial Cooperation in Criminal Matters).

questions.question: The Treaty of Nice (2001, in force 2003) was primarily designed to address:

options.A: The introduction of the euro as legal tender
options.B: Institutional reform to prepare for enlargement of the EU to include Central and Eastern European states
options.C: The creation of an EU Foreign Minister and diplomatic service
options.D: The incorporation of the Charter of Fundamental Rights into the Treaties

questions.explanation: The Treaty of Nice was driven by the need to reform the EU's institutions before the 'big bang' enlargement of 2004 (adding 10 new member states). It reformed the composition of the Commission, re-weighted votes in the Council under QMV, and adjusted the size and composition of the European Parliament and Court of Justice.

questions.question: The Constitutional Treaty of 2004 failed because:

options.A: The European Parliament rejected it by a large majority
options.B: It was rejected in referendums in France and the Netherlands in 2005
options.C: The UK refused to ratify it in Parliament
options.D: Germany's Federal Constitutional Court declared it incompatible with the Basic Law

questions.explanation: The Constitutional Treaty was signed in October 2004 but required ratification by all 25 member states. After initial positive ratifications, it was defeated in referendums in France (May 2005) and the Netherlands (June 2005), effectively killing the treaty. Most of its substance was preserved in the Lisbon Treaty (2007).

questions.question: The Treaty of Lisbon (2007, in force December 2009) made which of the following key changes?

options.A: Created the EU for the first time and introduced EU citizenship
options.B: Abolished the three-pillar structure, gave the EU legal personality, made the Charter of Fundamental Rights legally binding, and created the post of President of the European Council
options.C: Introduced the euro as legal tender across all member states
options.D: Established the principle of the supremacy of EU law over national law

questions.explanation: The Lisbon Treaty abolished the pillar structure (with special arrangements remaining for CFSP), gave the EU legal personality under Article 47 TEU, made the Charter of Fundamental Rights legally binding (Article 6(1) TEU), created a permanent President of the European Council and the High Representative for Foreign Affairs, and renamed the EC Treaty the TFEU.

questions.question: Article 50 TEU, which provides the procedure for withdrawal from the EU, was introduced by:
questions.explanation: Article 50 TEU was introduced by the Lisbon Treaty. Prior to Lisbon, the Treaties contained no express provision for withdrawal — the possibility of withdrawal under general international law principles was debated but uncodified in EU treaty law.

questions.question: The Lisbon Treaty renamed the Treaty establishing the European Community (TEC) as:

options.B: The Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU)

questions.explanation: The Lisbon Treaty restructured the two main treaties: the Maastricht TEU was amended and retained the name Treaty on European Union (TEU); the EC Treaty (formerly TEC, formerly EEC Treaty) was renamed the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU). These two treaties now have equal legal status.

questions.question: The 'big bang' enlargement of 2004 added how many new member states to the EU?
questions.explanation: On 1 May 2004, ten states acceded to the EU: Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Cyprus, and Malta. This was the largest single enlargement in EU history.

questions.question: The Lisbon Treaty's incorporation of the Charter of Fundamental Rights means:

options.A: The Charter replaced the European Convention on Human Rights for EU member states
options.B: The Charter has the same legal value as the Treaties, binding EU institutions and member states when implementing EU law
options.C: The Charter applies only to EU institutions and has no effect on member state conduct
options.D: The Charter is a declaratory document with no binding legal force

questions.explanation: Article 6(1) TEU gives the Charter of Fundamental Rights the same legal value as the Treaties. The Charter binds EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies, and binds member states when they are implementing EU law. Protocol 30 purports to limit its application in Poland and the UK (now irrelevant post-Brexit).

questions.question: Which of the following describes the 'ordinary legislative procedure' as introduced/consolidated by the Lisbon Treaty?

options.A: A procedure in which the Council legislates alone, with the Parliament having only a consultative role
options.B: A procedure involving joint decision-making by the European Parliament and the Council, with the Commission having the exclusive right of initiative
options.C: A procedure requiring unanimous agreement of all member states in the Council
options.D: A procedure by which the European Court of Justice approves legislation before it enters into force

questions.explanation: The ordinary legislative procedure (Article 294 TFEU), formerly the 'co-decision procedure', involves the Commission proposing legislation, with the European Parliament and the Council acting as co-legislators. It typically requires qualified majority voting in the Council and majority in the Parliament.

questions.question: The Lisbon Treaty created the post of High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy. What role does this post combine?

options.A: The President of the European Council and the President of the Commission
options.B: The former EU Foreign Policy representative and the Commissioner for External Relations, with the High Representative also serving as Vice-President of the Commission
options.C: The Presidency of the Council of the EU and the Secretary-General of the Council
options.D: The Commissioner for Trade and the head of EU military planning

questions.explanation: The High Representative 'double-hatted' role was created by Lisbon to provide greater coherence in EU external action: the HR chairs the Foreign Affairs Council, serves as a Vice-President of the Commission, and heads the European External Action Service (EEAS).
